I just want to make sure that I understand the situation.

To be able to use this package with crazyflie-clients-python, you would not be able to install the library in a virtualenv, because you would have to first ensure that the crazyflie-clients-python project itself could run in a virtualenv.

If I understand the situation correctly, we should probably open a ticket on crazyflie-clients-python to add virtualenv support. (which as noted may be challenging, I can explore more to see what we can do)  We should also provide instructions for installing crazyflie-lib-python globally to be able to use the client.

I think that this library by itself could be used by researchers and developers independently and should not be dependent on the client (the client should be dependent on the lib).  I would recommend showing how to use the examples provided with the lib with a virtualenv (in the way of steps on the README or scripts to support installation).

If you still feel that the only use-case for the lib is going to be the client, leaving the documentation about installing globally wouldn't really hurt things. (if you really must)

IMO, it's not worth even messing with a `BUCKET_NAME ` environment variable at all.  For someone to tune the value of an environment requires them to hack on a unit file and that just seems like a PIA compared to just setting the `bucketName` via deisctl.

How about this:

```
etcd_set_default bucketName registry
```

Then later in the boot script:

```
/app/bin/create_bucket $(etcdctl --no-sync -C ${ETCD} get ${ETCD_PATH}/registry)
```

We've been brainstorming a solution that would require no changes from users, though it does require two separate HTTPS connections (but I think TLS session caching would work). It should also be trivially portable to a kubernetes pod-style deployment.

TL;DR: Run an nginx instance on every machine in the cluster, which terminates a TLS connection from the router (not from the client), and reverse proxies on localhost to the correct container. This works for buildpack/Dockerfile/`deis pull` deploys.

## Using the core (non-k8s/mesos) infrastructure:

I'll build up from the app to the `deis-router`:

* The app you're deploying does not need to be altered.
* The `publisher` should continue publishing `hostAndPort` to `/deis/services/<app-name>`.
* Schedule a global fleet unit (call it `deis-local-router`) which runs nginx on every CoreOS machine and behaves a lot like the current `deis-router`, except:
    * Filter entries in `/deis/services/<app-name>` where `host:port` matches the hosts's IP
    * All upstreams are only on localhost
    * Only accept traffic on port 443 using a deis-internal TLS cert
* The `deis-router` keeps the same configuration except for ignoring the `:port` part of the `host:port` entry, and instead always upstreams to `host:443`, where `deis-local-router` is listening

The request path would then look like this:

```
client --(TLS w/ external cert)--> deis-router --(TLS w/ deis-internal cert)-+
                                                                             |
                              container:port  <--(localhost)-- host1:443  <--+
```

## Using kubernetes

This `deis-local-router` pattern should easily translate to an `nginx` container running alongside an app container in a `k8s pod`. I have not yet explored what might need to change to support this approach, though.

## Addressing open questions

I think this approach answers all of the open questions:

> Will application containers all include a self-signed cert and Nginx? Or just those that have opted into it?

The application containers won't need to worry at all about the cert or nginx, since a host-local nginx instance will handle that for them. All traffic received by the container will be unencrypted since it will be confined to localhost.

> If one opts into end-to-end HTTPS, can we expose the secure port in the application containers instead of the insecure port? Or, initially, do we expose it in addition to the insecure port? Proposing we shoot for the former; settle for the latter if necessary (for the first iteration).

Since the apps don't need to have knowledge of the backend security, always use the encrypted connection pattern that `deis-router-local` enables.

> How are routing and publishing affected? Publishing may or may not be affected depending on the previous bullet. Routing rules will be slightly different for anyone any app that opts into this.

Routing is affected by `deis-router` terminating public-facing TLS and starting a new TLS connection to the backend. `deis-router` would not route directly to the app's port on the target host, but instead directly to `host:443` which `deis-router-local` is listening to.

Publishing is not affected.

> How does this affect health checks and liveliness checks?

If this is a question about rolling/no-downtime deploys, these checks would all be confined to localhost in `deis-router-local`.

> How does it work with k8s as the scheduler? With k8s as the scheduler, publisher isn't really relevant. Router proxies service URLs. Those service URLs likely need to change for any app that has opted into this.

Pods deployed with a `deis-router-local` instance solve this well.

> How does this work for developers pushing their own Dockerfile or for those using deis pull?

Nothing needs to change because the TLS termination will still happen outside of the app container.

> if you'd like I'm more than happy to contribute to the documentation on the isolation of the planes

Absolutely! I'm thinking the Architecture section you already linked to could link to new page in Managing Deis where we could instruct operators on how to actually achieve separation.

> I could also provide a CloudFormation template for fast deployment.

We could perhaps include the changes/additions as a code snippet in the docs? I hesitate to include a completely separate CloudFormation template, as it'd have to receive updates whenever we update the one in contrib/ec2, and we really try to avoid updating code in multiple places.

When porting py-evm to Nim, we first used the same gas costs structure of Py-EVM but then refactored to isolate all gas costs and computation in a single file. See https://github.com/status-im/nimbus/pull/49 and the [final implementation](https://github.com/status-im/nimbus/blob/c9de0fbd29ad4559d64a467b68fdd1906bd34671/nimbus/vm/interpreter/gas_costs.nim#L508-L575).

In the end, we currently have on array per fork that maps the "name" to the gas cost from Yellow Paper Appendix G:

![2018-08-20_15-08-17](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22738317/44342321-e4733e00-a48a-11e8-9078-4f471ac50d4a.png)

```Nim
# Generate the fork-specific gas costs tables
const
  BaseGasFees: GasFeeSchedule = [
    # Fee Schedule at for the initial Ethereum forks
    GasZero:            0'i64,
    GasBase:            2,
    GasVeryLow:         3,
    GasLow:             5,
    GasMid:             8,
    GasHigh:            10,
    GasExtCode:         20,     # Changed to 700 in Tangerine (EIP150)
    GasBalance:         20,     # Changed to 400 in Tangerine (EIP150)
    GasSload:           50,     # Changed to 200 in Tangerine (EIP150)
    GasJumpDest:        1,
    GasSset:            20_000,
    GasSreset:          5_000,
    RefundSclear:       15_000,
    RefundSelfDestruct: 24_000,
    GasSelfDestruct:    0,      # Changed to 5000 in Tangerine (EIP150)
    GasCreate:          32000,
    GasCodeDeposit:     200,
    GasCall:            40,     # Changed to 700 in Tangerine (EIP150)
    GasCallValue:       9000,
    GasCallStipend:     2300,
    GasNewAccount:      25_000,
    GasExp:             10,
    GasExpByte:         10,     # Changed to 50 in Spurious Dragon (EIP160)
    GasMemory:          3,
    GasTXCreate:        32000,
    GasTXDataZero:      4,
    GasTXDataNonZero:   68,
    GasTransaction:     21000,
    GasLog:             375,
    GasLogData:         8,
    GasLogTopic:        375,
    GasSha3:            30,
    GasSha3Word:        6,
    GasCopy:            3,
    GasBlockhash:       20
    # GasQuadDivisor:     100     # Unused, do not confuse with the quadratic coefficient 512 for memory expansion
  ]

# Create the schedule for each forks
func tangerineGasFees(previous_fees: GasFeeSchedule): GasFeeSchedule =
  # https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-150.md
  result = previous_fees
  result[GasSload]        = 200
  result[GasSelfDestruct] = 5000
  result[GasBalance]      = 400
  result[GasCall]         = 40

func spuriousGasFees(previous_fees: GasFeeSchedule): GasFeeSchedule =
  # https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-160.md
  result = previous_fees
  result[GasExpByte]      = 50

const
  TangerineGasFees = BaseGasFees.tangerineGasFees
  SpuriousGasFees = TangerineGasFees.spuriousGasFees
  # Note that later forks are still WIP

# `gasCosts` is a macro that builds an array of gas cost functions
# It takes (Fee schedule, prefix of generated functions, result array of functions)
# as argument.
# The array is a property of vm forks.
gasCosts(BaseGasFees, base, BaseGasCosts)
gasCosts(TangerineGasFees, tangerine, TangerineGasCosts)
# Note that later forks are still WIP

proc forkToSchedule*(fork: Fork): GasCosts =
  if fork < FkTangerine:
    BaseGasCosts
  else:
    TangerineGasCosts
  # Note that later forks are still WIP
```

Quorom sets a higher `maximumExtraDataSize` than the standard 32 bytes.  The pythonic formatting middleware fails to process `extraData` because it expects the 32 bytes length.  This also occurs when connecting web3 to some other chains with other configurations, namely "geth --dev".

You can try adding the geth_poa_middleware to the middleware stack, which moves `extraData` to new field `proofOfAuthorityData` which does validate that the value is under 32-bytes.

```python
w3 = Web3(HTTPProvider('http://localhost:22000'))
from web3.middleware import geth_poa_middleware
# inject the poa compatibility middleware to the innermost layer
w3.middleware_stack.inject(geth_poa_middleware, layer=0)
```

Have a look at http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/middleware.html#geth-style-proof-of-authority

The plain address is  `0x8eb009288b68c343de5bc393a7e13c28f5425780`

The checksummed address is `0x8EB009288b68C343DE5Bc393a7e13C28F5425780`

Note the mixed case of the checksummed address.  The casing differences encode the checksum for the address.

The function `to_checksum_address` takes an address and returns the checksummed values.  The `upper()` call you link to is how it encodes the checksum in the return value.

The function `is_checksum_address` takes an address and returns `True` or `False` indicating whether it contains a valid checksum.  The comparison I think you are referencing **must** be case sensitive, otherwise it would return `True` for non-checksummed addresses.

I don't mind `CONDA_ROOT`.  It's sorta been [used in the past](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.0.x/conda/config.py#L129-L133) with a comment-warning that you really shouldn't use it.  

I'm using it officially now: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.x/conda/__init__.py#L30-L31

Setting it elsewhere shouldn't affect anything though, as long as you set it "correctly" (i.e. to an *actual* conda root).

The more time I spend on this (I've been hacking around for several hours this evening), the more I'm liking something along the lines of 

    export CONDA_ROOT=/home/me/anaconda
    # or:   setenv CONDA_ROOT /home/me/anaconda
    eval "$(conda init -)"

At that point, there are SOOO many things we can do to make our lives easier and our users lives better.  Things like `conda activate` [#341, #2080, #2337, #3111].  We don't have to get this ambitious yet, but *someday* we're going to have to communicate a change in the install procedure.  This is what it will be (or something similar) when we communicate it.  From there, users should never have to change again, because we have control of the code from there.

I'm waffling back-and-forth on whether we should just swallow `EACCES` when we try to write to that file.  I'm not sure we want to make its location configurable.  The reason is that, especially on unix, when you're UID `0`, `conda info --envs` (which should really be `conda list --envs` 😖) will look for that file in all known user home directories.  That way a sysadmin can discover all environments on a machine and scan environments for outdated packages (like e.g. openssl).

@krishnan-gopal It is important to note there is loose coupling between the Python and HDL world in VUnit. Basically the Python part provides a string to the test bench containing all configuration in a dictionary like structure. The HDL parts writes the results to an output file which is specified by the configuration string. Once simulation is done the Python side reads the output file and determines if test cases where passed, failed or skipped. The format of the output file is:
```
test_start:test1
test_start:test2
test_suite_done
```
All test pass if all tests start and test_suite_done is reached. A test is considered failed if it is the last test started and test_suite_done was not reached. A test is considered skipped if it was not started.

As for answers you requested:

> **Expected Behavior**
>
>Curator should successfully shrink the index. The debug logging should at least give a better understanding of why the moving was, or wasn't successful

The trouble here is that Elasticsearch has the answers in its logs, but the API call Curator uses only receives pass/fail type responses.  It cannot tell what happened in Elasticsearch, only that Elasticsearch says it worked, but then upon checking, the shards aren't there.

> **Actual Behavior**
>
>Curator unsuccessfully shrinks an index. The error states that not all shards are on the designated node, but does not provide cause or steps for resolution.

The most Curator could do here is effectively to say, "look in the Elasticsearch logs for the node(s) in question."  Curator makes API calls.  It's little more than an index-selection wrapper around the regular API calls.  If you were to attempt to allocate all shards to the same node manually, you might be able to see those in the logs in real time.

Thanks for letting us know about this issue.

The documentation for Put Block List REST API says:
"Note that this header is associated with the request content, and not with the content of the blob itself."

So passing in the MD5 of the whole blob to Put Block List is definitely the wrong thing to do.

We should calculate the MD5 for each chunk and pass it to Put Block (Put Blob in the case of no chunking), if the user wants validation.  The content\_md5 parameter should be changed to a Boolean, matching the behavior of the useTransactionalMD5 parameter used in Node.js SDK:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-node/blob/master/lib/services/blob/blobservice.js

All Page Blob and Block Blob APIs should be reviewed for this, both upload and download.

I wasn't considering it because `make` would get called more often than needed. The problem is that the hook usually gets somewhat inefficient for simple operations (such as amending the commit message) because the system `make` uses (comparing file modification timestamps) is not aware of `git` and could trigger entire rebuilds just because the developer switched to another branch. On the other hand, `pre-commit` does know when files have actually changed (if the file filters are properly set, of course).

In one particular case, which motivated this suggestion, I have a mixed-language repository where `make` builds only for a small portion of the code that is not often changed, and building it is a bit slow when compared to other hooks we use.

This sounds reasonable.

You _can_ hack it into the current scheme but it's not pretty nor very portable (without first-class support):

```yaml
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    sha: v0.7.1
    hooks:
    -   id: flake8
        entry: bash -c -o pipefail 'exec flake8 $@ |& tee -a flake8.log' --
```

To verify:

```
# important, since we use `-a` to append to the log (since pre-commit will invoke 1..many times)
rm flake8.log
pre-commit run flake8 --all-files || true  # if you want to demonstrate a failure writing to the log
cat flake8.log
```

For me (after breaking a file) this looks like this:
```
$ rm flake8.log 
$ pre-commit run flake8 --all-files 
Flake8...................................................................Failed
hookid: flake8

setup.py:1:1: F401 'os' imported but unused

$ cat flake8.log 
setup.py:1:1: F401 'os' imported but unused
```


As for first class, I think if you want to implement this I'd accept something like this:

```yaml
    -   id: flake8
        log_file: flake8.log
```

Note that pre-commit combines stdout and stderr, so you'd get combined stdout / stderr into that log file.

Ah, I can see what's happened here. It hasn't come up before.

Before piwheels, PyPI didn't accept Arm wheel uploads, so we automated building and hosting Arm wheels built from sdist of all packages on PyPI.

Since PyPI uploads moved to warehouse's back-end (long before the very recent front-end switch), Arm wheel uploads have been permitted.

Since Raspbian userland is 32-bit only, regardless on Pi model/CPU, all wheels built on a Pi 2/3 are identical to those built on Pi 1/Zero, despite the platform tag being armv6l/armv7l. But they're actually armv6 because of the agnostic userland.

We build "armv7l" wheels and provide duplicate files tagged as "armv6l" because they are compatible with all Pis in Raspbian. See [RTIMULib](https://piwheels.org/simple/RTIMULib/) for example.

When a compatible wheel is available on PyPI, all we do is download it, not build it from source. If this happens it is usually a pure python wheel which is not problem. However, in this case Armv7 wheels have been provided but not Armv6 (note: PyPI/warehouse does not allow uploading multiple identical files even with different tags like we do on piwheels).

> is piwheels repo set by default as a PyPi mirror on Raspbian?

It's configured as an *additional* index in `/etc/pip.conf`. If you remove this file, your requests will only hit PyPI. pip will choose wheels over sdist, so more often than not, pip will chose files from piwheels over PyPI.

> Does it takes precedence over PyPi if arm6 wheels are missing are there is only arm7 wheels?

Precedence is determined by the provider of the best matching file. Usually (e.g numpy) piwheels is preferred due to providing a wheel over PyPI's sdist, but in the case of a pure python package providing a wheel on PyPI (e.g. [gpiozero](https://pypi.org/project/gpiozero/#files)), they are equal and PyPI is preferred as the primary index.

If the user is on an "armv7" platform (Pi 2 or 3), pip will only match armv7 wheels. armv6 is simply not a match, the same way x86 isn't. So in this case, the user would get the armv7 wheel from pypi.

If the user is on an armv6 platform (Pi 1/Zero) and no armv7 wheel is provided on PyPI, pip's choice is between a seemingly valid platform wheel from piwheels vs. sdist from PyPI. piwheels wins.

However, if the original wheel is **true armv7** (as opposed to armv6 userland posing as armv7) we have a problem, because piwheels copied it to "armv6l" and is providing that to Pi 1/Zero users.

How was the armv7 wheel built? QEMU maybe? If it contains Armv7 instructions it will not work on Pi 1/Zero (and I'm not even sure about Pi 2/3).

Resolution:

- If the wheel is true armv7, does it definitely work on a Pi 2/3? If it was actually built by Raspbian, I have no idea what happened.
- If it works on a Pi 2/3 but not on Pi 1/Zero, can you rebuild on a Pi 1 and try to upload it to PyPI? If it differs from the armv7 wheel that will work fine - just let me know and I'll delete it from piwheels.
- If you remove `/etc/pip.conf` and pip install from PyPI on a Pi 1/Zero, does building from source work? If so, we can just blacklist this package from piwheels and not get in the way by trying to provide wheels.
- If building on a Pi 3 works, and a duplicate wheel for armv6 also works on Pi 1 then can you just upload sdist only in future and leave it to piwheels to build Arm wheels?

[`@minrk` commented](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/805#issuecomment-3382897): It seems to me that explicit nesting is _less_ complex than implicit nesting based on the level and location of header cells.  Isn't it easier to flatten a nested structure than to nest a flat structure?  I imagine we will need both notions internally, so should we not pick the one from which is is most sensible to make the other?

I also think performing actions on a group is more straightforward with nested structures (e.g. increase/decrease level, relocation, collapse/expand) because you invariably have the whole group as a single element in the structure, no matter what level.  When the list is flat, you have to reconstruct the group every time you perform group actions.

# Copyright (c) Jupyter Development Team.

MAINTAINER Luis Freire

USER root

# Install all OS dependencies for notebook server that starts but lacks all

RUN REPO=http://cdn-fastly.deb.debian.org \
 && echo "deb $REPO/debian jessie main\ndeb $REPO/debian-security jessie/updates main" > /etc/apt/sources.list \

    git-core \

ENV LANG en_US.UTF-8

USER $NB_USER

# Setup work directory for backward-compatibility
RUN mkdir /home/$NB_USER/work

# Install conda as jovyan and check the md5 sum provided on the download site
ENV MINICONDA_VERSION 4.3.21
RUN cd /tmp && \
    mkdir -p $CONDA_DIR && \
    wget --quiet https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-${MINICONDA_VERSION}-Linux-x86_64.sh && \
    echo "c1c15d3baba15bf50293ae963abef853 *Miniconda3-${MINICONDA_VERSION}-Linux-x86_64.sh" | md5sum -c - && \
    /bin/bash Miniconda3-${MINICONDA_VERSION}-Linux-x86_64.sh -f -b -p $CONDA_DIR && \

    $CONDA_DIR/bin/conda config --system --prepend channels conda-forge && \
    $CONDA_DIR/bin/conda config --system --set auto_update_conda false && \
    $CONDA_DIR/bin/conda config --system --set show_channel_urls true && \
    $CONDA_DIR/bin/conda update --all && \
    conda clean -tipsy

# Install node.js to build from source
RUN conda install -c conda-forge nodejs

USER root

# Install Jupyter Notebook from source
RUN \
    pip install --upgrade setuptools pip && \
    git clone https://github.com/jupyter/notebook && \
    cd notebook && \
    pip install -e .

EXPOSE 8888
WORKDIR $HOME

# Configure container startup
ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--"]
CMD ["jupyter", "notebook", "--ip=0.0.0.0", "--port=8888", "--no-browser"]

# Add local files as late as possible to avoid cache busting
RUN chown -R $NB_USER:users /home/jovyan/

# Switch back to jovyan to avoid accidental container runs as root
USER $NB_USER

When you shut down the console, it will normally clean up the connection file it created, so it won't be there for the next kernel to refer to.

What do you want it to do if you try to start two consoles at once? Use the same kernel, or refuse to start?

I think you could write a script that does something like this:

```bash
python -m ipykernel_launcher -f my_kernel.json &
sleep 1
jupyter console --existing my_kernel.json
```

That will try to start a new kernel using your connection file, and then open a console connected to the new kernel (or the old one if one already existed).

> In the IPython shell I run out of memory even when I'm not assigning the numpy arrays to anything:

When IPython displays an object, it keeps a reference to it so that you can do operations with it like `Out[5] + 2`. If the objects you're displaying are big, this means they will stay in memory. You can discard all this with `%reset out`, or delete a single object from IPython with `%xdel x` (where x is the variable name).

You can disable this entirely with the option [InteractiveShell.cache_size](http://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/config/options/terminal.html#configtrait-InteractiveShell.cache_size).

It sounds to me like it's the library (e.g. pandas) that decides how big of a mime bundle to return and that users should be configuring that there (e.g. `pandas.display.notebook_repr_html` or `pandas.display.html.table_schema`). If the model were on the server/kernel as @Carreau mentioned, the notebook could dynamically fetch parts of the mime bundle depending on the context (like rendering an output or saving a notebook file), but currently it's all in the browser so users will be expected to manage that to a certain extent and library authors will be expected to manage that as well. 

Another thing to consider is why libraries/kernels return multiple mime types for the same data in the first place. I think the primary reasons are for rendering  a) in notebook environments that don't have the necessary extensions installed and b) using nbconvert. I think that [binder](https://github.com/jupyterhub/binderhub) is going to change some of this. binder makes it really easy to distribute reproducible notebooks. I think that people will people will start to share notebooks via binder because it effectively eliminates reason A above and it provides users with a live notebook vs. a static HTML notebook (like on GitHub). I don't have any answer for nbconvert...it _could_ potentially render outputs using headless Chrome and capture static HTML for exporting to HTML, save screenshots for exporting to PDF, etc. @mpacer Is there any plan to support custom mime types in nbconvert?

I faced the same issue too. What was happening was that my ipython notebook was using the global instance of notebook installation since my newly created conda environment did not have jupyter notebook installed in it. And it was also searching for all the packages referenced in the notebook globally.

The fix was : conda install jupyter-notebook in the newly created environment.

After doing this, when I launched ipynb, it started using the packages in my environment and not the global ones. I also used conda install command for installing all the packages. 

To test whether it is a global/environment issue export your ipynb as .python file and try running it from bash. If it works in the environment's bash, you know that your notebook is trying to find packages in the global env.

This indicates that the client could not find a valid configuration file to pull server connection parameters from. The README has more information on the client configuration file:

https://github.com/OpenKMIP/PyKMIP#configuration

An example of the client configuration file can be found below; just copy that file to one of the expected configuration file locations (I recommend `/etc/pykmip/`) and fill in your client/server connection information.

https://github.com/OpenKMIP/PyKMIP/blob/master/examples/pykmip.conf

I agree that `add_state` or `add_to_state` are better.  However, the relation instances are not quite the same thing as state objects.  There isn't really a state object, as it's just a flag with some optional associated data (the relation instances). OK, I can confirm this is a CPU issue on the "server side" - that is, not the system that sshuttle runs on, but the sshd server that you point it to.

The pcEngines APU1D4 device I run my sshd on, with a "AMD G series T40E, 1 GHz dual Bobcat core with 64 bit support" maxes out at 7mb/s (but typically runs at 2-4 mb/s) and when I am connected, the single python process on the sshd server side maxes out at 100% of a CPU.

I tried pointing to a Celeron(R) CPU  N2807  @ 1.58GHz (1583.37-MHz K8-class CPU) and the results were not that much different, which makes sense as that is a fairly weak, old celeron chip.

But then I pointed to an sshd running on a CPU: Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5-2620 v3 @ 2.40GHz (2400.05-MHz K8-class CPU) and the speed increased all the way up to 22mb/s.  The single python process running on the sshd server ramps up to 50% of a CPU when I stress the connection.

So the speed of the CPU on the sshd server side matters quite a bit AND it needs to be a fairly new and powerful CPU - network devices like the APU or little router boards will not work.

My question:

The APU device I use has dual cores and is compiled with an SMP kernel ... but I think python does not support threading for workloads like this ?  I think I could at least double the speed on these weaker devices if there were a way to split the workload between more than one python thread ...

Here is a summary of the steps needed to create a python client which uses GSI authentication (in this case against an iRODS with GSI authentication enabled)

First of all you need to create a file which  maps the GSI OID to the Globus GSSAPI library:
   

    $ more  /etc/gss/mech.d/gsi.conf
    # GSI mechanism plugin
    #
    # Mechanism Name        Object Identifier               Shared Library Path                   
    gsi                     1.3.6.1.4.1.3536.1.1.1          /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libglobus_gssapi_gsi.so


Then in the client:
1.  Set the  authorization mechanism:
```
gsi_mech = gssapi.raw.OID.from_int_seq('1.3.6.1.4.1.3536.1.1.1')
```

2. Set the server name using the server X.509 DN:
```
server_name = gssapi.Name(<serverDN>)
server_name.canonicalize(gsi_mech)
```

3. Create the client context:
```
self.client_ctx = gssapi.SecurityContext(
                name=server_name,
                mech=gsi_mech,
                flags=[2, 4],
                usage='initiate')
```

4. Start the token exchange loop in _def handshake(self, target)_:
```
def send_gsi_token(self, server_token=None):

    # CLIENT TOKEN
    if server_token is None:
        client_token = self.client_ctx.step()
    else:
        client_token = self.client_ctx.step(server_token)
    logger.debug("[GSI handshake] Client: sent a new token")

    # SEND IT TO SERVER
    self.reply(len(client_token))
    self.socket.sendall(client_token)

def receive_gsi_token(self):

    # Receive client token from iRODS
    data = self.socket.recv(4)
    value = struct.unpack("I", bytearray(data))
    token_len = socket.ntohl(value[0])
    server_token = self.recvall(token_len)
    logger.debug("[GSI handshake] Server: received a new token")

    return server_token

def handshake(self, target):
    """
    This GSS API context based on GSI was obtained combining 2 sources:
https://pythonhosted.org/gssapi/basic-tutorial.html
https://github.com/irods/irods_auth_plugin_gsi/blob/master/gsi/libgsi.cpp
    """

    self.init_sec_context()

    # Go, into the loop
    self.send_gsi_token()

    while not (self.client_ctx.complete):

        server_token = self.receive_gsi_token()

        self.send_gsi_token(server_token)

    logger.debug("[GSI Handshake] completed")
```

The full code is available in the [python-irodsclient](https://github.com/irods/python-irodsclient) library.
Please feel free to reopen or bother us on freenode/#python-gssapi if you have further questions.

Setting group.id to None does not actually null it, it is converted to the string "None" - thus a valid consumer group. This is a bug in the Python wrapper.
However, the underlying librdkafka library will not allow you to run subscribe() on a consumer without a valid group.id set since the group.id is a required property for balanced consumer groups.
You can only use assign() when no group.id is set.

Why you are seeing an empty assignment initially is probably because of the above, you might have multiple clients using this "None" group and not enough partitions for all joined consumers. Sounds plausible?

@jbrockmendel This may be a bit complicated depending on what you want to do. I think mocking the `time` functions is probably the most portable and reliable way to go. When I originally started mocking out different time zones, I developed [context managers that temporarily change the system time zone](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/test/_common.py#L95) (which I'd like to expose with #438). I probably did it this way because I didn't know any better, but I'm not changing it today because I think that the fact that `tzlocal` is implemented with calls to the `time` functions is close enough to an implementation detail that I don't want to rely on it for my tests.

The main complication in doing it this way is that because of a bug in the compiler used to compile the Windows binaries for all versions of Python that I know about, the values from the `time` module are *not* dynamically updated during the run of a given Python executable, so you can't just change your time zone during a test and change it back and have that reflected by `tzlocal` (on Windows only), which is most of the reason why there's a separate `tzwinlocal` function (which pulls the values from the registry and *does* update during a given run). Obviously if you just mock the `time` module, you won't have this problem.

> b) Is there a list of valid tzlocal values (or time.timezone/time.daylight./time.altzone triples) for iterating over?

I'm not sure about this. Perhaps see what [hypothesis](https://github.com/HypothesisWorks/hypothesis-python) is doing in this regard? I suspect they are using `pytz` for their zones, so I dunno how well that would translate.

If you're comfortable digging into the internals of `dateutil` a bit, one way to generate a list of *plausible* `tzlocal` values would be to iterate over the `zoneinfo` files, instantiate a `tzfile` for each one and gather the set of unique (`tzname()`, `utcoffset()`, `dst()`) tuples.

Two questions @ocefpaf:

- any particular reason why you changed the `{ ... }` with `dict( ... )`? In general, there is no accepted convention for this, but people prefer in general to use the former. While this is debatable for multiline dicts, it seems the first is more practical / legible for stuff that is supposed to be in one line:
```Python
county_df = pd.DataFrame({"FIPS_Code": county_codes}, dtype=str)
county_df = pd.DataFrame(dict(FIPS_Code=county_codes), dtype=str)
```

and even more in the case of empty dicts (`{}` vs `dict()`). The literal syntax is also more efficient (http://doughellmann.com/2012/11/12/the-performance-impact-of-using-dict-instead-of-in-cpython-2-7-2.html).

- I also noticed you changed `Folium` to `folium` within docs. I would keep the name upperscore (as it is referenced in the the rest of the docs). If we want to change it, we need to do be consistent and change in all the docs through the whole codebase.

I agree `validator` would be more natural than `validate`, and `validate_with` is too long.

Currently, `validate` accepts both a callable and a collection of callables and I'd rather stick to only one version, either `validator` or `validators`.

    validators=my_callable

definitely looks weird.

    validator=(collection of callables)

could do, like @monotaro-yasushi-masuda says, it is a compound validator so singular form is understandable, but it is still surprising to provide a collection to a singular argument.

The most natural thing, of course, would be to use `validators` and accept only collections. Besides, internally the value is stored as `self.validators`, so it is clearer to expose this to the caller, so that the parameter matches the attribute like for the other attributes.

But most of the time we only use one validator, so the shortcut allowing a callable is nice.

There's a trade-off here between clarity and practicality (and history).

Keeping the ability to pass only a callable would be nice. In this case, `validate` comes in handy precisely because it does not indicate whether it is plural or singular.

At least, keeping `validators` inside does not break introspection for third-party libs (like apispec).

@rgant good point about the spec. I was probably too much focused on typical uses for such "meta" objects in most APIs I've seen so fat.

So we have at least two kinds of meta in spec:
1. document-level meta (top-level key): it can be used to provide information specific for whole request/response like debug information, total count of documents for multiple resource responses, copyright/authors (as exemplified in this issue). Generally all information that isn't specific to single resource if multiple entities available as list and should not be duplicated in every resource.
2. resource-level meta: can be used to provide meta information that is specific only for single resource object.

So to cover both cases it would be best to have two separate meta fields. E.g. `metadata/resource_metadata` or `document_metadata/metadata`. I still don't know what are typical use cases for resource-level metadata as I cannot imagine data that couldn't/shouldn't be expresses as attribute or relationship.

The handling of zenith near 90 in PVLib for Matlab was our addition to Hay and Davies.  An alternative  value for Hay and Davies at zenith = 90 would be the isotropic sky diffuse component, reasoning that at zenith = 90 there is not direct or circumsolar irradiance that reaches a tilted surface near ground level.  We could linearly interpolate from 89 (or 88) to 90. 

I am confident that the Hay and Davies model did not anticipate being applied at zenith = 90 and that we won't find any guidance in the original paper.

Another work around is to find the parameter assignments that are involved in the bug and reverse the order of the equality statement.

In the example you gave above, `src$2` and `dest$2` are the supposedly "hoisted" variables, I think because in the base case (rule 1), they are assigned to loop vars `src$1` and `dest$1` which in turn are untouched throughout the recursive case (rule 3). So to avoid detection by the unhoister, simply reverse the "assignment" in rule 1:
```
(= src$2 src$1)
(= dest$2 dest$1)
```
I guess the unhoister is expecting things to be written in a normalized form, as if emitted by a compiler? Indeed, a comment in the unhoister code indicates `The following mess is used to undo hoisting of expressions outside loops by compilers`.

> OK, sounds good. What should be changed to treat them as errors?

There are two places this could be done either as custom flags set in the TravisCI build scripts or as part of the CMake build system itself.

I'd prefer to do it in CMake itself because we can test the compiler at runtime and actually check whether or not it supports the warning we'd like to treat as an error.

This would likely be a modification to https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/cmake/compiler_warnings.cmake . We'd also probably want some sort of CMake option to not treat any warnings as errors just in case there's a bug in the compiler (e.g. certain versions of a compiler might give false positives) but have that off by default so that we treat the same warnings as errors during development as in TravisCI (provided the compiler being used for development supports the same warnings).

Currently `compiler_warnings.cmake` has an option to treat all warnings as errors but I think that's far too broad. It would probably better to maintain a list of warnings that we know aren't ever false positives.

You could apply a tactic to transform the current assertions into "simplified" form. For example, there is a tactic called "qe" that performs quantifier elimination. For your example, you have:

    (declare-const z Int)
    (declare-const y Int)

    (assert (=> (> z y) (exists ((x Int)) (> x y))))
    (apply qe)
    (apply (and-then qe simplify))

The results are not what you are expecting in the question because (exists ((x Int)) (> x y)) is equivalent to "true". Z3 produces the output:

    (goals
    (goal
      (=> (> z y) true)
      :precision precise :depth 1)
    )
    (goals
    (goal
      :precision precise :depth 2)
    )

The second set of subgoals contains no formulas, it is equivalent to "true".

Here is some more detail.

We already export all the information a C/C++ project (that uses CMake) needs when Z3 is built with CMake  in a file called `Z3Config.cmake`. The template for this file can be found at https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/cmake/Z3Config.cmake.in.

We generate two versions of this file. One that goes in the root of the Z3 build directory which allows an external project to use Z3 directly from a build tree (i.e. installing Z3 is not necessary). The other gets installed when Z3 gets installed (e.g. `ninja install`).

We have two example consumers of the `Z3Config.cmake` file. First the C example project https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/examples/c/CMakeLists.txt  and the example C++ project as well. This is how the C and C++ example projects are currently tested in TravisCI.

Hey guys -- I implemented my own version of Brian's paper in Lasagne and then discovered this version the other day. Glad to see I was on the right track since my implementation is almost identical. One thing I've noticed is that training with the cross-covariance cost can easily cause the gradients to blow up (producing nan's) pretty easily. For example, if I add dropout layers, change from adadelta to nesterov momentum schemes, etc. I think this is because the linear units in the encoder layer are unbounded and can therefore result in large values in the cross covariance computation. I see that Brian used a max_weight_norm penalty, which may be helping to stabilize things. Just wanted to add that I found a similar strategy that helps stabilize things by simply clipping the relu's in the pre-encoder layers to [0, 3].

The GCS API isn't 100% S3 compatible (if I recall correctly), and I'm pretty sure it breaks in S3FS because Boto3 uses Multi-Part uploads for larger files, and the GCS API doesn't support that. It works just fine for small files, but not all GCS features are supported.

(See https://github.com/PyFilesystem/pyfilesystem2/issues/147)

You can enable this using S3FS as-is, using the `endpoint_url` option. For example:

```
s3://BUCKET_KEY:BUCKET_SECRET@BUCKET_NAME/optional/path/to/directory?endpoint_url=https://www.googleapis.com
```

(I do this to write to Wasabi storage, which is 100% S3 compatible)

```
    def to_array(self, structured=False):
        """Convert the table to a NumPy array.

        If all columns in `self` have the same data type, then the
        output array will have this datatype. If there is > 1 data type
        in `self`, then the output will be of dtype `object`.

        This will strip information about the column names, it only retains
        each column's data.
        """
        arr = np.array(self.rows)
        if structured is True:
            # Pull data types / names for structured array
            dtypes = [col.dtype.name for col in self.columns]
            names = self.labels

            # Populate output array
            labels = [(iname, idtype) for iname, idtype in zip(names, dtypes)]
            arr = np.array(arr, dtype=labels)

        return arr
```

That single instruction is modeled as a "terminator" in CFGFast, using a `SimProcedure`. We treat it in the same way as any instruction that VEX does not like.

Ok, will do. Just checked the branch and register names are slightly off according to Power ISA version referenced by libvex, version 2.06.

The first 32 VSR registers (0-31) are also used as the floating-point registers (FPR# or F#), where the first dword is used to store the value. The vector registers (V#) are placed in the second 32 VSR registers (32-63). The PPC32 and PPC64 files in the `rewind` branch refer to the VSR registers with `vsr#` and `v#`, which seems incorrect.

See pages 274-275 (printed page number) in the [document](http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download;jsessionid=995FB78240B0A62F1629AB3454C3DFB7?doi=10.1.1.175.7365&rep=rep1&type=pdf) I shared earlier for more info.

Can you give me some more insight on what made bcp feel icky? My current usage does feel like a hack, but just curious if you had more details on that. I'll look into MS-TDS. Sounds like an interesting opportunity, I'll be curious to consider the scale since you mentioned it being a big project. Today I implemented an mssql CopyToCSV, that said I'm not in love with it. It works, but it feels like a hack, a lot of that is the bcp component.

startup.py
```
import sqlalchemy as sa
from odo import odo

metadata = sa.MataData(bind="mssql+pyodbc:///?odbc_connect%s" % params)
tbl = sa.Table('details', metadata, schema='scheme', autoload=True)
odo(tbl, 'bulk_out.csv')
```

odo/backends/sql.py
```
@compiles('CopyToCSV', 'mssql')
def compile_copy_to_csv_mssql(element, compiler, **kwargs):
    selectable = element.element

    connection_elements = selectable.bind.url.query['odbc_connect'].replace('=',';').split(';')
    connection_elements = dict(zip(connection_elements[0::2], connection_elements[1::2]))

    cmd_template = """bcp {database}.{schema}.{table} OUT {path} -S {server} -T -c -q"""
    cmd_statement = cmd_template.format(database=connection_elements['DATABASE'],
                                        schema=selectable.schema,
                                        table=selectable.name,
                                        path=element.path.replace('\\\\','\\'),
                                        server=connection_elements['SERVER'])

    os.system(cmd_statement)
    return ''

```
So there are a lot of reasons I don't like this. First it doesn't actually use sqlalchemy for anything but to be part of the compile decorated function pattern that's already present. Because of that it feels hacky, and I have to use the connection string to build up the bcp command. It's also just issuing a command on `os.system`, which isn't a bad thing, but very different from the other `compile_copy_to_csv_*` functions. Finally building up the bcp command is inflexible if somebody needed a different delimiter or flags then they would have to manually change this functions internals. 

What is nice is that for somebody using odo for sql -> to csv for a table export it's very fast, the usage is still just odo(mssql_source, csv_dest) and it routes with the sqlalchemy compiler which makes the discovery the same as the other sql.py CopyToCSV database functions. 

That said this was a first pass for me today just digging into the odo internals a bit more and seeing if I could get this working. I need to spend more time working with the odo graph and it's arrival at the `compile_copy_to_csv_*` functions. I have some notes from my conversation with Joe over the weekend to review where he had offered some suggestions and potentially a different way to route this instead of making it sqlalchemy compiled.

If you have any suggestions or concerns please let me know as I'm not married to this, but want to provide some form of working sql server bulk operation for odo. That may be MS-TDS I'll look into that this week and see what it is all about as well as exploring of sqlalchemy already has some MS-TDS extension available.

On further thought, I realized my [suggestion above](https://github.com/python-hyper/h11/issues/57#issuecomment-375221317) wouldn't actually handle the case that started this, because there the offending byte is inside a cookie, which means that the client has to be able to send it back to the server :-/. So I guess our options are:

- Loosen the header-value regex to accept any character except `\0`, `\r`, `\n`

- Be spec-compliant about what we send and receive in server mode, but use the loosened rule above in client mode. This would be somewhat annoying to implement, though, because of how h11 is structured (the same `h11.Request` or `h11.Response` objects are used by both clients and servers, they validate headers, and they don't know which role we're playing – if we were only changing the rules for parsing from the wire, that would be easier, because that's done by the connection object itself).

So I guess I'm now leaning towards the first option.

@egede The copy to temp and upload happens in the prepare step of the master job. There is a dedicated method for doing this from memory.

The fix for this bug needs to solve the following:

 1) The files copied to the tmp dir need to have a unique name for each job not for each prepared application. i.e. the folder should contain the job name not the prepared uuid.
 2) The RTHandler should copy inputfiles to this folder in tmp rather than the shared folder in the shared dir as information not common to the pre-prepared application will confuse things.
 3) We need to consider merging: #701 . Storing the uploaded file here without this will cause problems and increase the need to keep parsing strings into Ganga objects directly. (I was working to see this go, it adds averhead whenever dealing with a string within the schema for _all_ classes and this PR is the only thing which is getting in the way of this!)
 4) We can still store the uploaded information for a submitted job in the application as this is stored once per job object and the application is duplicated and unique upin assignment. It would be nice to move this information to the prepared state but it doesn't apply as the RTHandler intentionally uploads all files for the job in 1 upload to reduce the amount of DIRAC interaction we have.
i.e. There is no need to lock the uploading mechanism as this should be uploading a unique file to the grid and storing unique information within each job object.

 5) (Optional) we could separate the prepared files from those uploaded by the master job but this would require either #701 so we can store multiple files against the prepared state of a job or some other solution so that we can track the extra file somewhere in the schema.

In case you're using PNG renderer/interpreter to parse `_file_name_.raw` blob, then it will not work as PNG by design assumes that the binary data is ordered as big endianness.

Well, it's not just abuse. The problem is that `object_hook` requires going back and forth from the C encode to Python for every object being encoded.  This creates drag to the point where it would be unusable. You'd have a super fast bolt written using numpy, but then it'd spend 40% of its time doing JSON encoding.

That said, I'm not clear on @msukmanowsky's solution. If it's saying to let the user sort it out, I think that's perfect. Then you could figure out how best to encode the numpy array, without the overhead of using that specific json facility.  In fact, if we had an `object_hook`-like facility, we could build it in a generic sense, like this, but then perhaps figure out how to make a high-performance numpy-specific implementation layered on top.

Ah ok sorry, I think I see where the misunderstanding comes from now.

Relay is only one option with working with GraphQL on the client (others include Apollo as you mentioned and even slimmer clients like [urql](https://formidable.com/blog/2018/introducing-urql/)). Graphene supports all of these options out of the box and it also provides several helper classes to support Relay specifically (everything in the `graphene.relay` namespace).

The bit thats probably confusing you is that in the old version of Relay (relay-classic) it assumed that your GraphQL schema was setup in a particular way. For example it assumed you had a `node` field in the root query that allowed relay-classic to refetch a particular node whenever it needed to. This is what `relay.Node.Field` is for (more information can be found on the [relay docs](https://facebook.github.io/relay/docs/en/graphql-server-specification.html#object-identification)). With the new version of Relay (relay-modern) most of this is **optional** so you don't have to implement any of it in your schema.

Having said that you might still want to implement some of the relay schema **patterns** such as the `node` field and the [`connections`](https://facebook.github.io/relay/docs/en/graphql-server-specification.html#connections) specification because they turn out to be very useful even if you are not using relay. If that is the case then you can use the `graphene.relay` part of the graphene library but it is not required to start using GraphQL on your client.

I hope that clears some things up 🙂

You can download your the model need from url + '?tf-hub-format=compressed', such as 'https://tfhub.dev/google/imagenet/pnasnet_large/classification/2?tf-hub-format=compressed' . This file will be named 'x.tar.gz', then make two file in ‘/tmp/tfhub_modules’  named such as '67f40766542914aa2dd2ec6923c4f353f6d0663c'  and '67f40766542914aa2dd2ec6923c4f353f6d0663c.descriptor.txt' ,decompression 1.tar.gz to  67f40766542914aa2dd2ec6923c4f353f6d0663c.

For now one has to do it manually, example:

```python
import tensorflow as tf
import tensorflow_hub as hub

with tf.Graph().as_default():
  module = hub.Module("http://tfhub.dev/google/universal-sentence-encoder/2")
  text = tf.placeholder(tf.string, [None])
  embedding = module(text)
  
  init_op = tf.group([tf.global_variables_initializer(), tf.tables_initializer()])
  with tf.Session() as session:
    session.run(init_op)
    tf.saved_model.simple_save(
        session,
        "/tmp/serving_saved_model",
        inputs = {"text": text},
        outputs = {"embedding": embedding}        
    )
```

Each modules differs slightly from others in input/output names, additionally each serving use case might have different requirements (e.g. raw features in vs serialized tf.Example protos). By having users creating the graph they want to serve (e.g. as done above) seems more flexible than require users to guess what Servo config to use and/or modify the client side each time they change the module being served.

That's right - embed(messages) will build a part of the TensorFlow graph. Graph building can be slow but graph execution is typically fast.

To embed many sentences, you can either enlarge the "messages" list. Another option is to use

```
messages = tf.placeholder(dtype=tf.string, shape=[None])
```

and then you can give a value for the placeholder several times (e.g. in a loop) without incurring any graph building overhead:

```
message_embeddings = session.run(output, feed_dict={messages: [some_sentence]})
```

That will build the graph once and run it several times.

Hi, the reason it always output [1.0] is due to the fact that accuracy is not suitable for evaluating continuous values, it was designed for classification. R2 can be used for continuous values, but it requires X and Y to have same shape. 
For regression over continuous values, loss itself is already a meaningfull metric, but according to your specific case, you can write you own metric (see https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/metrics.py#L64 for an example, and use that class into regression layer `metric` argument).

```python
tx_transfer = bdb.transactions.prepare(operation='TRANSFER',inputs=input_,asset=transfer_asset,recipients=rob.public_key,)

Notice how `inputs=input_` and `input_` is the same `input_` as was used when transferring to Bob, but it should be different because that input is supposed to fulfill a different output.

(CREATE tx) --> (TRANSFER tx 1) --> (TRANSFER tx 2)

When preparing (TRANSFER tx 1) you were fulfilling an output on (CREATE tx), but when preparing (TRANSFER tx 2), you are fulfilling an output on (TRANSFER tx 1).

For anyone who is wondering: EV certs are "extended validation certificates", the issuing CA goes through extra steps verifying the identity of the requesting entity. Letsencrypt cannot do this. LE issues DV (domain validation) certs, meaning it has just validated that the requesting entity owns the domain for which the cert will be issued. Only EV certs are approved for .onion sites, we need DV certs to be approved for certbot to have .onion support.

There was some discussion about this on the CA/Browser forum mailing list. The thread is broken up, I'll try to summarize:

Alec Muffett asks how to get a Onion-capable SSL cert for his blog. He can't get an EV cert as an individual. Explains why he needs this. ([link](https://cabforum.org/pipermail/public/2015-November/006212.html))

Ryan Sleevi responds explaining the decision to limit issuance to EV certificates:
* There are questions about the cryptographic strength of the .onion naming scheme. It uses RSA-1024 and SHA-1, both of which should be considered deprecated.
* A cyrptographic attack on the .onion naming scheme would compromise DV certificates, but not EV.
* CAs believe it is their responsibility to police content by revoking certificates in the name of "protecting the users". Allowing unattributed certificates is contrary to this (?). 
([link](https://cabforum.org/pipermail/public/2015-November/006213.html))

Paul Syverson responds to Ryan's explanation:
* The naming scheme is transitioning to SHA-256 and ed25519.
* Even without that, demonstrating ownership of a .onion "stronger" than demonstrating ownership of a registered domain over http.
* CAs can revoke the certificate of an onionsite just as they could for a registered domain.
([link](https://cabforum.org/pipermail/public/2015-November/006250.html))

Ryan Sleevi responds to Paul. He explains how with the Tor service descriptor extension and certificate transparency, a cryptographic attack on the onion naming scheme based on factoring RSA-1024 or a SHA-1 collision could be mitigated with EV certificates but not DV. But the introduction of DV certificates could allow for downgrade to DV attacks. And how catastrophic one of these attacks would be. The oninonsite could never again be trusted, ever. Ryan does not agree with CAs belief they should police content, but they must be dealt with amicably in the CA/Browser forum.
([link](https://cabforum.org/pipermail/public/2015-November/006251.html)).

(end thread)

Has there been any more recent work lobbying the CA/Browser forum?

What is the state of updating the tor network? If I understand correctly, onionsites created with RSA-1024 will *always* be vulnerable because the name is based on the key. And you would have to create a whole new key, and therefore name, if you wanted to use ed25519.

Unfortunately, I'm not familiar with ClearOS, but I'll tell you what certbot-auto is doing on RHEL 6 based systems and maybe you can tell me what's going wrong. In our last major release, we deprecated support for Python 2.6. Now when you run certbot-auto on RHEL 6 based systems, it tries to install Python 3.4 from [EPEL 6](https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/EPEL), letting `yum` prompt you to agree/disagree with this.

The way this works is it first checks if EPEL needs to be enabled. It does this by running:
```
yum list *virtualenv
```
If this command exits with a zero status, we assume EPEL is already enabled. If it does not, we either error out asking you to enable EPEL or try and do it ourselves letting `yum` prompt for your permission.

I suspect our detection of EPEL is off on ClearOS because `yum` as run by certbot-auto is not finding the Python 3 packages available in that repo. The packages it's looking for is python34, python34-devel, and python34-tools.

I'd like to get more information from you so we can try and work around this problem automatically in the future, but as a workaround you could try making sure the EPEL 6 packages are available to `yum` and try running `certbot-auto` again.

# setting objective function (not too readable)

model.setObjective(
  quicksum(a*x[i])/b[i] for i in range(0,len(Xitems)))
  + quicksum(i*c[i]*d*y[i]) for i in range(0,len(Yitems)))
  + quicksum(i*i*z[i])/b[i] for i in range(0,len(Zitems))) , "minimize")
```

As you can see, the line for the objective function is becoming pretty long. I would rather have something like this:
```
# defining x contribution
xContrib = model.addVar(vtype="CONTINUOUS")
model.addCons(xContrib == quicksum(a*x[i])/b[i] for i in range(0,len(Xitems))))

# defining y contribution
yContrib = model.addVar(vtype="CONTINUOUS")
model.addCons(yContrib == quicksum(i*c[i]*d*y[i]) for i in range(0,len(Yitems))))

# defining z contribution
zContrib = model.addVar(vtype="CONTINUOUS")
model.addCons(zContrib ==  quicksum(i*i*z[i])/b[i] for i in range(0,len(Zitems))))

# setting up the objective function in a more readable way by using these intermediate variables
model.setObjective(xContrib + yContrib + zContrib , "minimize")
```

> If I understand correctly, this patch allows to have more than one decision task, which the case of a retrigger.

You do understand correctly.  We also allow for more than one decision task in any other case, and retriggering is one of those cases.

> Do we also allow a completely different decision task to be injected in the graph?

Sure.  This would might be helpful for something like Fennec release promotion: one decision task creates the releasetasks graph, and at the end of that we want to trigger the `mach taskgraph` nightly fennec graph in another decision task.

> If so, does that allow somebody to plug tasks at the end of the taskgraph? Or to insert tasks in the middle?

Good question!  Yes and no.

- By default, `mach taskgraph` will generate a new graph; the decision task will be the only task in the old taskgraph.  Essentially, this means the new decision task has the old decision task's `taskId` as its `taskGroupId`, but all the tasks that the new decision task spawns will have the new decision task's `taskId` as their `taskGroupId`.  That means we verify those new tasks against the new decision task.

- If the new decision task inserts non-decision tasks at the end of the old taskgraph, and those are tasks where we verify the chain of trust artifact, then "at the end of the old taskgraph" means their `taskGroupId` matches the *old* decision task's `taskId`, and we'll verify these tasks exist in the old decision task's `task-graph.json` artifact.  If these are essentially retriggers of previous tasks in the graph, this will work.  If the task definitions have changed at all in a meaningful way, chain of trust verification will fail.

- There is no real way to insert tasks into the "middle" of the old taskgraph.  This essentially means that tasks in the previous graph will rely on artifacts from the new tasks.  We should be explicit about downloading artifacts from upstream `taskId`s, in which case someone would have to replace the definition of that task or the artifacts from that task to MitM the graph.  In the case of overwriting old indexes with new artifacts, that can be done anyway outside the graph; we shouldn't implicitly trust the indexes as they exist today.

ATM we [allow transfers](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/channelgraph.py#L183-L184) with nodes in the "unknown" network state, which is the [default value](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/protocol.py#L310-L315) when the healthcheck is starting. This definitely explains what you experienced.

The network state is changed to "unreachable" after 20 seconds ([2 retries of 10 seconds each](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/settings.py#L25-L26)). This may happen either because the ethereum node is [lagging behind](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1020#issuecomment-330422761) or the network is dropping packets.

@alykhantejani 

1. A 3-channel image could be grayscale if R == G == B
[Example image from ImageNet](http://farm1.static.flickr.com/132/387212290_4573a5f996.jpg)
[Grayscale in RGB](https://www.w3schools.com/colors/colors_shades.asp
)
2. Origins
- Dataset might contain such 3-channel images like the example shown above

- If dataset has single channel images, they need to be converted to 3 channel as pre-trained models are available only for 3 channel. This is usually done by replicating across channels. This leads to the same situation

3. Why is it needed ?
Labeling of dataset could be expensive (example FACS, emotions etc) and we want to make use of both the colored and grayscale images (instead of throwing away one set).  For introducing, invariance to grayscaling of an image, we could employ an augmentation where we randomly (with a probability p) grayscale an image. The idea is that over multiple epochs, the network sees an image as well as its grayscale counterpart (which has the same label) and learns the invariance during backprop accordingly.

> Or should we just not touch this and say that the current behavior is acceptable?

Yep - I think we should just not touch this.  Once we've addressed the issues noted in https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1532#issuecomment-404003295, we could reconsider.  But for now this is totally fine as is.

> Just curious, will this work even if you are missing a tsconfig.json file?

It would not have, but with 6e3ba8924246a1dd33437ba4fd0a0a9f40c465b1 it now does.  We set the following compiler options (this is what we set in our existing `tsconfig.json`'s):

```
compilerOptions: {
  target: "es6",
  module: "commonjs",
  moduleResolution: "node",
  sourceMap: "true",
},
```

Any additional options in a `tsconfig.json` will be merged in.  If you want to change one of the above, you'll have to fall back to using tsc directly, since ts-node prefers the options we explicitly set to ones in tfconfig.json

Introducing a new function to reset HTTP session state seemed like a good idea to me for several reasons:

1. The internal state of one of the Streamlink object's member variables is modified. In the spirit of data encapsulation, it should be the Streamlink object that does this (managing the HTTP session state ought to be the Streamlink object's task; its module name is even streamlink.session).
2. streamlink uses a custom subclass of requests.Session. Should in the future some state be added to this class that needs to be reinitialised when the HTTP session is, the new_http_session() function is easier to find than an equivalent instruction in a plugin.
3. Resetting HTTP session state may be useful to other plugins, too -- its use is not restricted to misbehaving HTTP servers.

Some thoughts from talking to others:

Checkpointing doesn't need to be focused on storing the actual value of parameters.

We need some way of, given a function call that's about to happen, discover if there's "the same" function call in the checkpoint database already.

One way is to compute a canonical form for all of the parameters and store that as a byte sequence. Another way might be to compute a sufficiently robust hash, and store that as a byte sequence.

Doing this for arbitrary python types seems hard; but for individual specific python types, it may often be easy.

Checkpointing at present does not duplicate work, so is safe from a referential transparency perspective; but it is unsafe in the sense that if you believe you've checkpointed a large piece of work, you may find yourself not using that saved in a subsequent run.

Being able to indicate to the user that checkpoints are unsafe for a particular workflow/invocation would be nice (beyond writing it in the documentation)

Misconfiguration in the urls.py. It seems i18n_patterns are not compatible with the package:

Incorrect:
```
if 'markdownx' in settings.INSTALLED_APPS:
    urlpatterns += i18n_patterns(
        url(r'^markdownx/', include('markdownx.urls'))
    )
```

Correct:
```
if 'markdownx' in settings.INSTALLED_APPS:
    urlpatterns += [
        url(r'^markdownx/', include('markdownx.urls'))
    ]
```

Wanted to quickly follow up, since I am not sure what you meant by the _deep end_. How and whether you would use pyecore all depends on how the UML model is represented (instantiated) in you CMS usecase. 

Do you already have Python classes for the different UML types like class or association? If so I don't think there is much gain in using pyecore, as it would allow you to build the UML metamodel you already have. Then, to generate XML from it I would probably use Python's etree library, i.e. use model-to-model (UML objects to etree nodes) transformation code to build the output DOM and then serialize to XML text using etree.

Alternatively, you could use the [pymultigen](https://github.com/moltob/pymultigen) project (basis for pyecoregen) to build a code generator for a model-to-text (UML objects to XML file) transformation. In that case you could write a Jinja2 (or any other kind of) template that essentially produces XML text as you want it.

Your UML class instances probably represent some form of business entities. You could use pyecore to model these entities and their relations and then generated XML from that, as described above.

If your UML model is not even in memory, but represented as text, you need to use a parser (or write one) to get a memory representation of it and then continue as above.

But when you say CMS, web applications usually come with their templating environments, so you can probably use that one directly to produce XML text from UML objects that are passed to the template's context. In that case, pyecore and agx would be overkill in my point of view.

Maybe this helps a bit, feel free to ask for more input in case you want to go a certain route.

Your concern is exactly what happened to me without this change when receiving results. Previously the json type is MicroDict but none of the sub objects are. So I added a line which will convert returned dicts to MicroDicts.

The only difference between a MicroDict and a dict is the __getitem__ call, a MicroDict attempts to resolve something like 'EmailAddress' as 'emailAddress' first, if that fails, then it tries 'EmailAddress', basically trying the first character as lower, then upper if that fails.  

The MicroDict doesn't actually modify any keys in the dictionary, just tries to get them using upper and lowercase variants, therefore it shouldn't have any affect on requests sent to APIs.

Yes, this is what I expected, but reading the code, I didn't see anything that would suggest that there is *anything* in this handler that differs from tornado, and I didn't want to go make a diff myself.  Keeping in sync with tornado is hard, so we need to document very clearly what our changes are when we are doing clobber&copy.  I think you should writeup separately, or at least document inline quite thoroughly where the ZMQ subclasses are copied and changed from tornado, so we can see what it's doing, and why what tornado itself does cannot be made to work with existing APIs.

> The Monkey patching was not originally for the user's benefit but to enable the existing parts of tornado to pick up the right handlers. These handlers are used in tornado.web in methods we don't override - we need those methods to pick up our versions.

Okay, that makes sense, but is not what the comment suggests to me.  In that case, I would just leave it as-is with a more detailed comment explaining that these names are purely for use in the classes in this module, and not to be imported by anyone (e.g. the should be excluded from `__all__`).

> Do we want to present zmq.web as a complete and API compatible version of tornado.web?

No, I think this is a bad idea.  Monkeypatching is never good, and should be avoided whenever possible.  A simple subclass approach, as we are doing here makes perfect sense.
